narrative:
Markets remain in a business-as-usual regime with a stress score of 25, reflecting a calm and orderly backdrop. The slight five-day drawdown in equities of 0.7 percent sits well within normal trading noise at a z-score of minus 0.53, indicating nothing exceptional. This benign positioning is anchored by stable credit conditions and contained volatility expectations.

The VIX sits at 16.8, placing it at the 35th percentile of its ten-year range, which is solidly below the historical median and consistent with calm conditions. The term structure remains in normal contango, offering no warning signals of imminent stress. High-yield spreads at 272 basis points are exceptionally tight at the 8th percentile with a z-score of minus 1.11, while investment-grade spreads at 73 basis points are similarly compressed. Credit markets are confirming the equity picture: there is no hidden stress lurking beneath the surface.

The weakness in Chinese equities stands out as the main sectoral story, with FXI down 3.6 percent over five days alongside a broad retreat in cyclicals. Materials fell 3.2 percent and industrials declined 2.3 percent, suggesting some caution on growth momentum. This is offset by strength in Germany, up 1.9 percent, and modest resilience in energy and UK equities, indicating regional divergence rather than systemic concern. Only two of eleven sectors are in the red and just two of eight emerging markets are declining, so breadth remains solid.

No equity dislocations or deep value opportunities have been flagged at this time.
